steps.text: Remove gloves and other PPE in a manner that minimizes personal contamination and transfer of infectious materials outside of the areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed or manipulated.
Substitute plasticware for glassware whenever possible.
Uncap needles in a manner that reduces the potential for recoil causing an accidental needlestick.
Use a hands-free device or comparable safety procedure (e.g., a needle remover on a sharps container, or forceps) to remove or recap a needle.
Place used, disposable needles and syringes in puncture-resistant containers immediately after use.
Place non-disposable sharps in a hard-walled container for transport to a processing area for decontamination.
Remove broken glassware using a brush and dustpan, tongs, or forceps.
Perform all procedures carefully to minimize the creation of aerosols or splatters of infectious materials and waste.
Decontaminate work surfaces after completion of work and after any spill or splash of potentially infectious material with appropriate disinfectant.
Decontaminate all cultures, stocks, and other potentially infectious materials.
Place materials to be decontaminated outside of the immediate animal room in a durable, leak-proof container and secure for transport.
Disinfect the outer surface of the container prior to moving materials and ensure the transport container has a universal biohazard label.
Pack materials to be removed from the facility for decontamination in accordance with applicable local, state, and federal regulations.
Obtain CDC and/or USDA importation permits.
Obtain a domestic transport permit from USDA APHIS VS, if required.
Obtain a DoC permit for the export of this agent to another country, if required.
Register with the Federal Select Agent Program (FSAP).
Obtain prior approval from FSAP.
Obtain a DoC permit.
Close the APR doors completely without damaging them when entering or exiting.
Perform a site-specific risk assessment to assess the benefits of installing a second APR door between the animal room exit and the door used to exit the biocontainment facility.
Conduct a risk assessment to determine if pneumatic doors should be equipped with redundant seals.
Check and adjust mechanical compression seals at regular intervals to ensure full contact when the seal is engaged.
Seal and verify airtight all integral features of APR doors through pressure decay testing.
Perform a factory acceptance test that simulates anticipated impact load to ensure the door unit will meet minimum load requirements.
Perform pressure decay testing to confirm that HEPA filter frames, housing, and the ductwork between the ABSL-3Ag facility and the HEPA filter are airtight.
Identify and validate methods for effectively decontaminating sections of potentially contaminated ductwork that extends outside the ABSL-3Ag facility.
Install pre-filters (at least 80–90% efficiency) in air supply and exhaust ductwork, preferably inside the biocontainment room or facility.
Disinfect or decontaminate used pre-filters by a validated method.
Keep plumbing traps filled with liquid disinfectant or capped.
Install HEPA filters on return lines of pneumatic systems (e.g., plumbing vents, pneumatic lines for inflatable door seals, vacuum systems).
Install a HEPA filter near the service cock or point of use.
Evaluate energy recovery from common systems that serve laboratories with varying (low and high) loads during operation.
Review energy efficiency and flexibility when evaluating fume hoods.
Consider allowing manifold exhaust.
Conduct a Computational Fluid-Dynamics (CFD) Model to evaluate airflow patterns.

preconditions.text: It is absolutely necessary to remove a needle from a syringe or recap a needle.
A spill procedure is developed and posted within the animal facility.
An effective integrated pest management program is required.
If exporting this agent to another country
Importation of this agent requires CDC and/or USDA importation permits.
Domestic transport of this agent may require a permit from USDA APHIS VS.
A Department of Commerce (DoC) permit may be required for the export of this agent to another country.
Entity possesses, uses, stores, or transfers BoNT-producing species of Clostridium, or quantities of BoNT above the permissible amount.
Domestic transfer or importation of BoNT-producing species of Clostridium or BoNT above the permissible amount is intended.
Export of these agents and toxin to another country is intended.
Following any structural modifications to the facility
At regular intervals determined through a site-specific risk assessment while the facility is in use
If APR doors are at risk of being physically damaged by large animals
Prior to removing used pre-filters from the ABSL-3Ag facility for disposal
When a vacuum source is needed

constraints.text: Eating, drinking, smoking, handling contact lenses, applying cosmetics, and storing food for human consumption are not permitted in laboratory areas.
Food is stored outside the laboratory area.
Needles must not be bent, sheared, broken, recapped, removed from disposable syringes, or otherwise manipulated by hand before disposal.
Do not handle broken glassware directly.
The sharps disposal container must be located as close to the point of use as possible.
Spills involving infectious materials are contained, decontaminated, and cleaned up by staff who are properly trained and equipped to work with infectious material.
Animals and plants not associated with the work being performed are not permitted in the areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed or manipulated.
BSL-2 practices, containment equipment, and facilities are recommended for all activities involving the use or manipulation of known or potentially infective tissues, body fluids, and cultures.
ABSL-2 practices are recommended for the housing and manipulation of infected animals.

Primary laboratory hazards include ingestion, parenteral inoculation, and direct and indirect contact of skin or mucous membranes, particularly the conjunctiva, with cultures or infected tissues or body fluids.
Individuals with valvular heart disease should not work with C. burnetii.
Work with C. burnetii should be avoided during pregnancy.
All rules concerning the possession, storage, use, and transfer of Select Agents apply.
Some Rickettsia spp. can be infectious by an aerosol route, thus adherence to BSL-3 practices is essential.
Parenteral inoculation/needlestick injuries are also among the more common routes of laboratory infection.
Infections can also be acquired by conjunctival inoculation.
SE are highly toxic by intravenous and inhalation routes of exposure, with lethal doses causing death in NHPs mainly due to shock and/or pulmonary edema.
Exposure of mucous membranes to SEB in a laboratory setting or in clinical studies has been reported to cause conjunctivitis and localized cutaneous swelling.
Access to containment areas should be restricted to authorized personnel.
All entry and exit points should be secured with locks or equivalent electronic access systems and protected by alarms.

The entrance to the ABSL-3Ag containment facility should have a double door vestibule that separates containment areas from non-containment areas, with doors mechanically interlocked to prevent simultaneous opening.
When two doors are interlocked, at least one of the doors must meet air pressure resistant (APR) specifications.
Personnel must be trained to close the APR doors completely without damaging them.
Pneumatic lines that inflate the gaskets on APR doors should be equipped with HEPA filters and check valves.
Extreme negative pressures can develop if fast-acting dampers are used in closed rooms instead of redundant supply fans, which can injure personnel and animals or cause structural damage.
Used pre-filters must be regarded as contaminated.
Exhaust air cannot be recirculated to supply non-animal areas.
Heated air must be directed towards the laboratories where the exhaust air came from to minimize the potential for any cross-contamination in the event of a leak within the transfer system.
Do not use commercial identification systems (manual or automated, including MALDI-TOF and Vitek) for the identification of suspected Select Agents.
There is a risk of generating aerosols during operation of MALDI-TOF.
Wear closed laboratory coat or gown, eye protection, closed-toe shoes, and gloves in the clinical laboratory.
Use respiratory protection when working with an infectious agent that is known or suspected to be airborne transmissible.
Protect mucous membranes (eyes, nose, and mouth) when working with an infectious agent known to be transmissible by blood.
